title: I am enough as I am today
subtitle: A three-minute affirmation practice for the days the not-enough sentence is running.
excerpt: A short affirmation practice for the days when the not-enough sentence is running underneath everything. Hand on heart, three repetitions in the morning, three in the evening. One sentence; the work is the repetition.
sourceNotes: Affirmation craft is public-domain practice, popularised in modern form by Louise Hay (You Can Heal Your Life, 1984). The present-tense / positive-construction shape follows the received craft. The specific sentence and its use as a daily practice are original to the homemade.education library.

practice.whenToUse: Use on the days when the not-enough sentence is running — when nothing you've done feels like it counts, when you find yourself listing what you haven't done before what you have.

content.text: A three-minute affirmation practice for the days the not-enough sentence is running underneath everything else. One short sentence, said three times in the morning, three times in the evening, hand on heart. The work is the repetition over days and weeks; the practice itself is small.

The not-enough sentence is one of the loudest sentences a body holds — somewhere between an inherited message and a daily-worn-in pattern. It runs underneath the to-do list, the comparison to other women, the way you measure your own day. The affirmation is the counter-sentence the practice rehearses.

attrs.quote: I am enough as I am today.

content.text: Three times in the morning. Hand flat on the centre of the chest. Say the sentence out loud if the room allows it, in your mind if it doesn't. Slow. Let each word land before moving to the next.

Three times again in the evening, the same way. Or at the moment in the day when the not-enough sentence usually lands — opening the inbox, scrolling other people's lives, comparing your morning to someone else's afternoon.
Stay with this one sentence for two weeks at least. The work is the wearing-in of the new pattern; switching to a new sentence every morning means none of them land.

If the sentence feels too far away on a particular day, or if "enough" reads as a lie, drop to a smaller truer version. "I am here today." "I am doing what I can today." Find the version the body accepts. The smaller true sentence beats the bigger one you can't say.

Affirmation craft is public-domain practice, with roots in the New Thought movement of the late 19th century and modern-form popularisation by writers like Louise Hay (You Can Heal Your Life, 1984). The present-tense / positive-construction shape used here follows the received craft. The specific sentence and its use as a daily practice are original to the homemade.education library.
